title: A framework for thinking about the potential public health ...
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Discussion
Content:
We have provided a framework for describing the potential impact of an epidemiologic question anchored to the magnitude of the health problem it seeks to address—in terms of number, severity, and distribution of cases; the potential to shift the health problem—in terms of the nature of the intervention and prevalence of exposure and other component causes; and research context. We hope it will be helpful for researchers as they frame their questions and communicate the public health importance of their work.

title: A Framework-Based Approach to Assessing Mental Health Impacts ...
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Discussions
Content:
*Step two* is to understand the “risk pathway” (i.e., how sequential events or responses that lead to mental health consequences) through the conceptual ecological framework consisting of microsystem, mesosystem, exosystem, macrosystem, and, finally, chronosystem.

title: Conceptual frameworks for the integration of genetic and social epidemiology in complex diseases - ScienceDirect
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Introduction
Content:
Theory and conceptual frameworks help epidemiologists evaluate the causes of health and disease, thereby influencing research questions, collaborations, study design and interpretation of findings  [4 , 5 ]. While long-standing social epidemiological theories, such as Ecosocial theory and Fundamental Cause theory, describe hierarchical relationships between multi-level determinants of health , these frameworks do not deliberate on the role of genomics and other -omics on health outcomes  [6 , 7 ]. Similarly, the field of genetic epidemiology has not fully embraced the use of conceptual models that could place genetics in the context of other determinants of health.

title:
A framework for thinking about the potential public health impact of epidemiologic research
      -  Johns Hopkins University
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Cite this
Content:
An epidemiologic question need not be impactful along all these factors to make answering it worthwhile. However, answering epidemiologic questions with more of these factors present will likely have greater public health impact than answering questions for which these factors are absent. We hope that collecting these factors into a single framework may aid students and senior epidemiologists alike when organizing arguments for the value of their own work or attempting to evaluate the impact of others{\textquoteright} work.",
